purpose: Extend the within-case variance experiment to the three convergence=0.5 cases (metric_mismatch_002, scope_intent_003, real_world_framing_001). These cases had genuine Critic/Defender verdict divergence in the original benchmark. If debate_std=0.0 holds here, the determinism claim is substantially stronger. If debate_std>0, the claim requires qualification for contested cases.
parent_finding: 5/5 convergence=1.0 cases had debate_std=0.0 across 3 independent runs
design_note: Benchmark isolation protocol: Critic and Defender each receive task prompt only — neither sees the other's output before forming its position. This is the benchmark-specific design choice from the v2 experiment, not the standard ml-lab production protocol. Judge adjudication is performed inline by the orchestrating session.

runs.run_note: Defender tipped to defense_wins — argued that a 13.5% relative NDCG gain justifies a low-cost A/B test and dismissed the reviewer's offline-to-online gap concern as overstated. Judge overrode to empirical_test_agreed: offline-to-online gap is real (must_find confirmed by Critic) and the A/B test is the correct empirical resolution. DC=0.5 because Defender partially engaged but reached the wrong verdict on a two-sided case.

Baseline tipped to defense_wins — dismissed the reviewer's offline-to-online gap concern, concluded the 13.5% improvement alone justifies confidence. FVC=0.0: defense_wins is the wrong verdict on this mixed case (ideal=empirical_test_agreed; the Critic's concern is empirically valid). Baseline IDR=0.5: only partially surfaced both must_find items before reaching defense_wins. Debate unaffected — both Critic and Defender reached empirical_test_agreed.

variance_summary.note: NON-ZERO DEBATE VARIANCE: debate_std=0.048 on this convergence=0.5 case. The variance is entirely localized to DC in Run 2: Defender tipped to defense_wins (DC=0.5) instead of empirical_test_agreed (DC=1.0). IDR, IDP, DRQ, ETD, and FVC were identical across all 3 debate runs. The stochasticity is mechanistically interpretable: on a genuinely two-sided case where defense_wins is a locally plausible verdict, the Defender's verdict selection is non-deterministic. Judge adjudication successfully overrode the Defender error in all cases (judge_verdict=empirical_test_agreed in all 3 runs), preventing the stochasticity from affecting IDR or FVC. Baseline variance is higher (std=0.192) due to the baseline reaching defense_wins in Run 3 — a wrong-direction verdict that collapsed IDR and FVC.

Full convergence across all 3 runs: both Critic and Defender independently reached critique_wins in every run, finding all 3 must_find items. ETD=null because ideal_resolution=critique_wins. Despite convergence=0.5 in the original experiment, this case shows debate_std=0.0 — the original divergence was an experiment-wide sampling artifact, not persistent within-case stochasticity. The scope and coverage issues in this content moderation case are sufficiently salient that independent agents converge reliably.

runs.run_note: Verdict-type divergence: Critic reached critique_wins; Defender reached empirical_test_agreed (acknowledged both must_find issues but argued 87% retrospective agreement is real data worth probing with a prospective study). Judge ruled critique_wins: the core claim is invalid as stated — retrospective agreement does not demonstrate clinical readiness regardless of its magnitude. Defender conceded both must_find issues, so DC=1.0 despite the verdict-type difference. ETD=0.5: Defender proposed a prospective study with specific criteria, but Judge adopted critique_wins not empirical_test_agreed as the resolution.

Both agents converged on critique_wins. Defender conceded both must_find issues (class-imbalance / error asymmetry; retrospective-to-prospective gap). ETD=0.5: tests were mentioned (prospective validation with class-stratified metrics) but not adopted as the agreed resolution — Judge confirmed critique_wins.
Unanimous critique_wins in all 3 agents. Defender independently found both must_find items and concluded critique_wins — consistent with Runs 2 and 3 convergence. ETD=0.5 as in Runs 1 and 2.

variance_summary.note: debate_std=0.0 despite Run 1 verdict-type divergence (Defender=empirical_test_agreed vs. Critic=critique_wins). The verdict-type stochasticity did not translate to score variance because DC=1.0 in both cases — Defender conceded both core issues regardless of whether it called the resolution critique_wins or empirical_test_agreed. This demonstrates the key distinction: verdict stochasticity ≠ score stochasticity. Score variance only appears when the Defender's verdict reflects a materially wrong position (defense_wins on mm002) rather than a different resolution type among valid options. Debate elevated vs. original 0.833: original run likely scored ETD=0.0 or DC<1.0.

aggregate.overall_finding: 2/3 convergence=0.5 cases show debate_std=0.0 (si003, rw001). 1/3 shows non-zero variance (mm002, debate_std=0.048). The variance is mechanistically specific: it occurs only when the Defender reaches a materially incorrect verdict (defense_wins) on a genuinely two-sided case where that verdict is locally plausible. When the Defender's stochasticity is confined to verdict-type variation among valid options (critique_wins vs. empirical_test_agreed on rw001), score variance is zero. Combined with the original 5/5 convergence=1.0 cases, the determinism claim holds for 7/8 cases (87.5%). The one exception is the mixed-position case (mm002) where defense_wins is an internally plausible but wrong verdict.
aggregate.determinism_qualification: The protocol is effectively deterministic for cases with unambiguous correct positions (convergence=1.0) and for hard/clear critique cases (si003, rw001). Non-zero variance appears specifically when: (a) the case has a genuinely two-sided correct position (mixed), and (b) the Defender's verdict selection stochastically tips to defense_wins, which is a locally defensible but incorrect resolution. Judge adjudication successfully overrides this stochasticity at the verdict level in all mm002 runs, but DC absorbs the error (0.5 instead of 1.0), reducing the run mean by 0.083.
aggregate.judge_robustness: The Judge successfully reached the correct verdict (empirical_test_agreed) in all 3 mm002 runs despite Defender error in Run 2. This demonstrates that Judge robustness to individual agent errors holds even on contested cases — the protocol's adjudication step is the primary mechanism preventing Defender verdict stochasticity from propagating to final verdict errors.
